name: Recife
summary: Recife is considered one of Brazil's most dangerous cities for tourists, with high rates of violent crime, robbery, and theft. While the city offers vibrant culture, beautiful beaches, and rich history, visitors must exercise extreme caution and take comprehensive safety precautions.
quickVerdict: Recife presents significant safety challenges for tourists. The city has one of Brazil's highest crime rates, with frequent armed robberies, muggings, and violent crimes affecting both locals and visitors. Foreign tourists are particularly targeted, especially around beaches, hotels, and tourist areas. While many travelers visit safely with proper precautions, this destination requires constant vigilance, avoiding nighttime activities, and staying in well-secured areas. Consider this an advanced travel destination requiring extensive safety awareness.

neighborhoods.name: Boa Viagem
neighborhoods.description: The main tourist beach area with better infrastructure and police presence, but still experiences regular carjackings and robberies even during daylight hours. Stay in major hotels and avoid the beach at night.

neighborhoods.name: Olinda
neighborhoods.description: Historic colonial town with UNESCO World Heritage status. Generally safer during the day with tourist police presence, but avoid wandering alone and stick to main tourist areas. Evening visits should be in organized groups.

neighborhoods.name: Recife Antigo
neighborhoods.description: The historic downtown area is vibrant during business hours but becomes extremely dangerous after dark and on weekends. Multiple security incidents reported involving tourists. Visit only during busy daytime hours.

neighborhoods.name: Casa Forte
neighborhoods.description: Upscale residential neighborhood with shopping centers and restaurants. Relatively safer than downtown areas but still requires standard urban precautions. Good for dining and shopping during the day.

neighborhoods.name: Pina
neighborhoods.description: Mixed area near the airport and port. High crime rates with frequent reports of robberies and assaults. Avoid unless absolutely necessary for transit purposes, and use reliable transportation.

neighborhoods.name: Favelas/Peripheral Areas
neighborhoods.description: Informal settlements with extremely high crime rates, drug trafficking, and violence. Strongly advised against visiting even with guides, as neither tour companies nor police can guarantee safety.

scams.name: ATM Skimming
scams.description: Criminals install skimming devices on ATMs and point-of-sale terminals to steal card information, particularly at gas stations, hotels, and tourist areas. Fake terminals are used to collect credit card data.
scams.howToAvoid: Use ATMs inside banks during business hours, cover your PIN entry, check for suspicious devices attached to card readers, and enter wrong PIN first to test if machine is compromised.

scams.name: Fake Police Scam
scams.description: Criminals dress as police officers and approach tourists asking to inspect documents and bags, looking for drugs or contraband. Once you hand over valuables, they flee with your belongings.
scams.howToAvoid: Real police typically only ask for ID documents. Ask to see their badge and credentials if they want to search bags. You have the right to refuse searches and ask to go to a police station.

scams.name: Distraction and Theft
scams.description: Teams of criminals distract tourists while accomplices steal phones, wallets, or bags. Common methods include asking for directions, spilling something on you, or dropping items at your feet to pick up.
scams.howToAvoid: Stay alert in crowded areas, keep valuables secure and out of sight, be wary of overly friendly strangers, and trust your instincts if something feels off.

scams.name: Express Kidnapping
scams.description: Criminals abduct victims for short periods to force them to withdraw money from ATMs or pay quick ransoms. Sometimes involves taxi drivers or people offering help.
scams.howToAvoid: Use only official taxis or ride-sharing apps, never accept rides from strangers, avoid displaying wealth, and inform others of your whereabouts when out alone.

soloFemale.overview: Solo female travel in Recife is extremely challenging and not recommended for inexperienced travelers. Women face significant risks including catcalling, harassment, and being targeted for crimes. Many travel safety experts specifically advise against women traveling alone to Recife due to high crime rates and cultural factors that can make solo female travelers vulnerable.
tips: Never travel alone at night or use public transportation after dark
Stay in well-secured hotels in Boa Viagem area with good reviews
Dress conservatively and avoid displaying expensive items
Join group tours rather than exploring independently
Keep someone informed of your location at all times
Consider hiring a reputable local guide for sightseeing
Avoid beaches and downtown areas when not busy with other tourists

nightSafety.overview: Recife is extremely dangerous at night, with crime rates spiking significantly after dark. Even well-lit tourist areas like Boa Viagem experience regular robberies and violent crimes. The downtown area becomes virtually abandoned at night and is considered a no-go zone. Many locals avoid being out after sunset unless absolutely necessary.
tips: Avoid all outdoor activities after dark
Use only secure hotel transportation or pre-arranged rides
Stay within hotel premises during evening hours
If you must go out, travel in groups and stick to busy, well-lit areas
Never walk on beaches after sunset
Use taxis or ride-sharing apps rather than walking
Keep hotel contact information and emergency numbers readily available

transport.metro: Recife has a limited metro system that is generally considered unsafe, especially at night. Stations can be crowded and pickpocketing is common. Use during daylight hours only and stay alert for theft. Service is limited and may not reach all tourist destinations.
transport.rideshare: Uber and 99 Pop are widely available and considered the safest transport option. Always verify driver and vehicle details before getting in. Use in-app payment rather than cash. Wait for rides inside buildings when possible rather than on the street.
transport.taxis: Official white taxis with blue stripes are available but ensure the meter is running. Airport taxis charge fixed rates. Avoid unlicensed taxis and never accept rides from strangers. Radio taxis are safer than street hails, especially after dark.
transport.tips: Avoid public buses due to high robbery risk. The US State Department advises government employees against using municipal buses due to serious safety concerns. For airport transfers, pre-book reliable transport or use ride-sharing apps.

customs: Brazilians are warm and friendly, greeting with handshakes or cheek kisses (beijinhos) depending on familiarity and gender
Punctuality is relaxed for social occasions - arriving 15-30 minutes late is acceptable and even expected for dinner invitations
Daily showering and personal hygiene are extremely important cultural norms - Brazilians will notice if you skip daily showers
Tipping 10% is customary in restaurants if not already included in the bill as a service charge
Finish all food on your plate as leaving food is considered impolite and suggests you didn't enjoy the meal

health.overview: Recife has moderate health risks with mosquito-borne diseases being the primary concern. Healthcare quality varies significantly between private and public facilities, with private hospitals offering much better service.
health.water: Tap water quality varies and bottled water is recommended for tourists. Avoid ice in drinks unless you're certain it's made from purified water.
health.vaccinations: Yellow fever vaccination is NOT required for Recife specifically, as the city is not in a yellow fever risk zone. Routine vaccinations should be up to date. Consider hepatitis A, typhoid, and chikungunya precautions.
health.altitude: Recife is at sea level with a tropical climate. High humidity and temperatures year-round. Main health risks include dengue, chikungunya, Zika from mosquitoes. Use insect repellent consistently.

emergency.usEmbassy: US citizens can contact the US Consulate General in Recife at +55 (81) 3416-3050 during business hours, or the US Embassy in Brasilia at +55 (61) 3312-7000 for after-hours emergencies

faq.a: Recife has significant safety challenges and is considered one of Brazil's most dangerous cities for tourists. While many people visit safely, it requires extensive precautions, constant vigilance, and avoiding many areas and activities. First-time visitors to Brazil should consider other destinations first.

No, Recife is extremely dangerous at night. Crime rates spike significantly after dark, even in tourist areas like Boa Viagem. Downtown areas become abandoned and unsafe. Visitors should stay in secured hotels and avoid all outdoor activities after sunset.

faq.q: Is Recife safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Solo female travel to Recife is strongly discouraged, especially for inexperienced travelers. Women face high risks of harassment, targeting for crime, and dangerous situations. If visiting alone, stay in secure hotels, join group tours, and never venture out alone at night.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Recife?
faq.a: Avoid downtown Recife at night and weekends, all favela areas, peripheral neighborhoods, empty beaches after dark, and public transportation at night. Even tourist areas like Boa Viagem require caution. Stick to busy, well-lit areas with other tourists present.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Recife?
faq.a: Public buses are considered high-risk due to frequent robberies and assaults. The US State Department advises against using municipal buses. The metro system has limited coverage and safety issues. Ride-sharing apps like Uber are the safest transport option.
